title: Photo corner mounting
subtitle: Attaching photographs and cards to scrapbook pages without adhesive contact
excerpt: Photo corners are small triangular pockets that hold the corners of a photograph or card against a page without any adhesive touching the photograph itself. The photograph can be removed and repositioned later. Acid-free photo corners are the archivally correct mounting method for irreplaceable photographs in memory books and scrapbooks.
sourceNotes: Photo corners as a mounting technique for photographs predate the scrapbooking movement and are documented in photography album and conservation literature. The acid-free variety entered wide use in archival contexts in the latter twentieth century. Technique synthesised from photographic album and archival mounting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small triangular pocket, self-adhesive or gummed, that slides over the corner of a photograph and is then adhered to the page, securing the photograph without any adhesive touching it.
A material with a neutral or slightly alkaline pH that will not produce acids over time. Acid-free photo corners and mounting materials are essential for the long-term preservation of photographs.
A mounting arrangement in which the photograph sits free of the page background on all four sides, with a visible gap between the photograph edge and the page or mat behind it.

content.text: are the standard mounting method for photographs that may need to be removed or repositioned later. Unlike direct adhesive, they contact only the very corner of the photograph, leaving the image surface and the majority of the reverse entirely untouched.
photo corners will not discolour the photograph edges over decades; standard sticky corners with acidic adhesive will.

Photo corners come in two types: self-adhesive (peel-and-stick backing) and gummed (moistened to activate the adhesive, like a postage stamp). Self-adhesive corners are quicker to apply but sometimes slip if the backing paper is very smooth. Gummed corners adhere more reliably to difficult surfaces. Choose white or clear corners for photographs where you do not want the corner visible; black corners are a traditional aesthetic choice for black-and-white photograph albums. Size the corner to the photograph: an oversized corner overwhelms a small print; an undersized corner may not hold a large or heavy card.

Place the photograph in its intended position on the page and mark the corners lightly with a pencil dot at each corner, on the page, just outside the photograph edge. Remove the photograph. These four dots give the target corners for placing the photo corners. Apply the first photo corner to the page at the top-left mark, peel the backing if self-adhesive, and press firmly. Apply the top-right corner. Slide the photograph into the two applied corners to confirm alignment, then apply the two lower corners around the photograph's lower corners.

positions the photograph so that a gap is visible between its edges and the background or mat below. Apply the photo corners further from the image than for a flush mount: if the photo corners are 5 mm deep, position the corners so the photograph sits 3 to 4 mm away from the pencil marks on all sides. The visual effect is that the photograph appears to hover above the page, which can work well for single featured images.

items.symptom: The photo corners peel away from the page after a few weeks
items.cause: The page surface is too smooth or slightly waxy, and the adhesive on the photo corner is not bonding adequately.
items.fix: Use gummed corners instead of self-adhesive, or apply a small drop of PVA under the corner before pressing down. For archival work, use a bone folder to burnish each corner firmly for a full thirty seconds after application.

items.symptom: The photograph slides out of the corners
items.cause: The corners are slightly too large for the photograph, allowing it to shift when the album is moved.
items.fix: Use corners that fit the photograph edge snugly. Alternatively, apply four corners (one per corner) rather than just two diagonal corners, which distributes the holding force to all four sides.
